content: Gout is an inflammatory crystal arthropathy caused by deposition of monosodium urate crystals in joints and tissues due to chronic hyperuricemia (serum urate >6.8 mg/dL - saturation point). Hyperuricemia results from decreased renal excretion (90% of cases) or increased production. Risk factors: male sex, obesity, alcohol (especially beer), high-purine diet (red meat, seafood), fructose-sweetened beverages, medications (thiazides, loop diuretics, niacin, cyclosporine, tacrolimus), CKD, metabolic syndrome, genetic variants (URAT1, GLUT9). Clinical phases: asymptomatic hyperuricemia; acute gouty arthritis (sudden severe pain, swelling, erythema - classically first MTP joint 'podagra'); intercritical gout (asymptomatic between flares); chronic tophaceous gout (urate deposits in joints, bones, soft tissues, ears). Tophi pathognomonic but late finding. Gout associated with increased cardiovascular and renal disease risk.
